title: Unit 03 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Macroeconomics and Economic Indicators.

questions.prompt: What does Gross Domestic Product (GDP) measure?

choices.text: The total value of final goods and services produced within a country over a specific period of time
choices.feedback: Correct. GDP measures total economic output produced within a country during a period of time.

choices.text: The total amount of money saved by households each year
choices.feedback: Savings can affect the economy, but GDP measures production, not total savings.

choices.text: The number of people who are unemployed
choices.feedback: Unemployment is a separate economic indicator and is not the same as GDP.

choices.text: The change in prices over time
choices.feedback: Price changes over time describe inflation, not GDP.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is one of the main components of GDP?

choices.feedback: Voting participation is not a GDP component.
Correct. Consumption is one of the four major components of GDP.
Weather can affect the economy, but it is not a GDP component.
Hobbies are not a formal component used to calculate GDP.

questions.prompt: Frictional unemployment can happen when people are changing jobs or entering the workforce.

choices.feedback: Correct. Frictional unemployment is often short-term and happens during job transitions.
This is incorrect. That statement describes frictional unemployment.

choices.text: Unemployment caused by seasonal weather changes only
choices.feedback: Seasonal patterns may affect jobs, but that does not define structural unemployment.

choices.text: Unemployment caused by workers and jobs not matching because of changing skills or technology
choices.feedback: Correct. Structural unemployment happens when workers' skills do not match available jobs.

choices.text: Unemployment caused by rising prices of groceries
choices.feedback: Rising prices describe inflation, not structural unemployment.
Exports are not the defining cause of structural unemployment.

questions.prompt: Inflation reduces purchasing power when prices rise faster than income.

choices.feedback: Correct. When prices rise and income does not keep up, the same money buys less.
This is incorrect. Inflation can reduce purchasing power when prices rise faster than income.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows inflation affecting the cost of living?

choices.text: A student studies longer for a test
choices.feedback: Studying longer is not an example of inflation or cost of living.

choices.text: A family notices that rent and groceries both cost more than they did last year
choices.feedback: Correct. Rising prices for basic expenses are a clear example of inflation affecting the cost of living.

choices.text: A store sells one product at a discount for a weekend
choices.feedback: A single temporary discount does not describe inflation.

choices.text: A company builds a new factory
choices.feedback: Building a factory relates more to investment than to inflation.

questions.prompt: Which phase of the business cycle is marked by declining economic activity?

choices.feedback: Expansion is a period of rising economic activity, not decline.
Recovery happens when the economy begins improving after a downturn.
Correct. A recession is a period of declining economic activity.
Peak is the high point before activity begins to slow.

questions.prompt: If GDP is increasing and unemployment is falling, what does that most likely suggest about the economy?

choices.feedback: Rising GDP and falling unemployment usually do not suggest a shrinking economy.

choices.text: The economy is showing signs of growth
choices.feedback: Correct. Higher output and lower unemployment generally suggest economic growth.

choices.text: The economy has reached a trough
choices.feedback: A trough is the low point of the cycle, which does not fit rising GDP and falling unemployment.
GDP and unemployment do not show that inflation has disappeared.
